title: Harness Lab
subtitle: Workshop operating system for working with AI agents
principles: Work so another team can continue

phases.label: Opening and orientation
phases.goal: Open the day as a shared start: why harness engineering matters right now, what the room will do from the first beat to the reveal, and how we will know the work can stand without improvised rescue.
phases.roomSummary: The room should feel immediately that this is not a prompting hackathon. It is a day of steering agents, building with them in a real repo, and later testing what survives without us.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest. We are starting a day of steering work with coding agents so something durable stays behind.
You will learn, build, hand off, and inherit. That day arc is the point, not workshop logistics.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and the next team can continue safely.
Whatever stays only in table talk will not survive the afternoon.
watchFors: The opening slips into rules before it offers ambition and a reason to care.
The room reads the day as a prompting contest or a feature race.
The facilitator explains the continuation mechanic too early instead of simply setting the bar for continuation.
checkpointQuestions: Where would the next team find this without you?
What is actually verified here?
What should the room carry into Build Phase 1?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Open the day as a shared room start, not as an operating brief.
say: We are not starting with a tool demo or a prompting contest.
You will learn, build, hand off, and inherit. That arc is the point of the day.
We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where the agent and the next team can continue safely.
show: Run the launch arc from framing through analogy and room activation into the first working contract.
Show the participant surface only briefly at the end of opening or in the talk bridge.
do: Do a fast room grouping by current experience with AI agents and let two short voices land.
Keep the launch brisk: each beat should change the room state, not explain the whole workshop.
watch: The opening must not turn into an internal operating memo.
The room should feel the ambition of the day before it gets the first rule.
fallback: If time slips, keep the framing, the analogy, and the first contract. Shorten the activation to a quick show of hands.
If energy drops, return to the line that the afternoon will test what remains without rescue.

scenes.label: Opening promise
scenes.title: Today we are building a working system, not prompt theatre
scenes.body: The goal today is not one impressive answer. The goal is to learn how to steer work with coding agents so the repo, workflow, and instructions still hold without your running commentary.
facilitatorNotes: Start calm, but with ambition. The first beat should say that this is a build day with coding agents, not a tool onboarding session. [NARRATING]
Say harness engineering explicitly, but do not stay inside the definition. Move quickly into the day arc: learn, build, hand off, continue.
After the main line, pause briefly and let it land before you move into the day arc.

blocks.title: Today we are building a working system, not prompt theatre
blocks.body: Harness engineering means shaping instructions, context, verification, and workflow so the agent and the next team can continue safely. Today we are not just describing that system. We are building it.

blocks.title: The main line for today
blocks.body: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a working system that carries the next move without table-side explanation.

blocks.title: What should change today
blocks.body: This is a day that should change how you work with coding agents after the workshop too. Not a feature race. Not a contest.

scenes.label: Day arc
scenes.title: The day has one arc: learn, build, hand off, continue
scenes.body: The morning does not begin with a rules card. It begins with a promise that you will experience the full arc of agent work in one day.
facilitatorNotes: Keep this as the promise of the day, not as workshop logistics. [NARRATING]
Give each step one sentence. The room should feel that everything ahead has one direction.
Close by naming that the afternoon tests what the morning actually encoded in the repo.

blocks.title: What you are actually going to experience today

items.title: Learn to steer the agent
items.body: Not as a trick, but as a working discipline around context, boundaries, and verification.

items.title: Build something real
items.body: In a repo, with a plan, verification, and first artifacts another team can actually continue.

items.title: Hand it off
items.body: In the afternoon, to another team without oral rescue from the authors.

items.title: Carry it back to work
items.body: In the reveal, you name what belongs in your real workflow next week.

blocks.title: Why the launch matters
blocks.body: We are not opening a process. We are opening a day that should change how the room works with coding agents after today.

scenes.label: Lego duck analogy
scenes.title: The same bricks, a different duck
scenes.body: Give people the same bricks and you still will not get one correct duck. The same is true here: the model alone does not determine the quality of the work. Context, boundaries, and team imagination do.
facilitatorNotes: Use the Lego-duck analogy briefly and concretely. This is not a cute detour. It explains why harness engineering is a creative discipline. [TEACHING]
Do not ask who is right. Ask what produced the more useful working result and why.

blocks.title: What stays the same and what changes the result

items.title: The same model
items.body: The ingredients may be similar, but that alone does not guarantee useful work.

items.title: The same task
items.body: Without framing and boundaries, people and agents still build results with very different continuation value.

items.title: A different harness
items.body: The deciding difference is context, verification, and how legibly you design the next move.

blocks.title: Context is not decoration
blocks.body: The agent does not determine the quality on its own. The working system around it does.

scenes.label: Room activation
scenes.title: Stand where you honestly are with AI agents today
scenes.body: I want a quick map of the room: daily users, careful practitioners, curious beginners, or sceptics. This is not a ranking. It is a facilitation map for the day.
facilitatorNotes: Do not turn this into long self-introductions. A quick move, two short voices, and then back to the main line. [FACILITATING]
The point is not seniority. The point is to make the workshop participatory and to calibrate the room in public.

items.title: I use AI agents almost daily
items.body: I want to test what here is actually transferable into team workflow.

items.title: I use them, but carefully
items.body: I care about where to trust them and where to build stronger boundaries.

items.title: I am still near the start
items.body: I want a clear working model, not only a tour of possibilities.

items.title: I am sceptical, but I want evidence
items.body: I am watching for what survives handoff and verification.

blocks.title: Why do this at the start
blocks.body: This workshop is not only projection. It is work with a real room, its current habits, and its current pace.

scenes.label: Day schedule
scenes.title: Here is what the day looks like
scenes.body: Before the room moves, everyone should know the shape of the day: when things happen, what kind of work each block asks for, and where the key turning points are.
facilitatorNotes: Walk through the schedule quickly — one sentence per block, no commentary. The room just needs orientation, not motivation. [NARRATING]
Highlight the rotation at 13:30 explicitly. That is the moment most people will not expect.
Do not explain what harness engineering means here. That is the job of Context is King. Just name the blocks.

items.title: 09:10 — Opening
items.body: Framing, day arc, and room activation.

items.title: 09:40 — Context is King
items.body: Why context beats prompting. A live contrast exercise.

items.title: 10:05 — Demo
items.body: A working agent workflow from AGENTS.md to review.

items.title: 10:30 — Build Phase 1
items.body: Teams build in their repos. The longest block of the morning.

items.title: 11:35 — Intermezzo 1
items.body: Pause, reflect, recalibrate.

items.title: 12:15 — Lunch
items.body: Eat and prepare for the handoff.

items.title: 13:30 — Rotation
items.body: Teams swap repos. You continue someone else's work.

items.title: 13:45 — Build Phase 2
items.body: Build in the inherited repo without the original authors.

items.title: 14:45 — Intermezzo 2
items.body: What survived the handoff? What broke?

items.title: 15:45 — Reveal
items.body: Reflection and commitments for Monday.

blocks.title: The afternoon tests the morning
blocks.body: Everything you build before lunch will be read and continued by a different team after lunch. That is not a surprise — it is the design of the day.

scenes.label: Team start board
scenes.title: The day starts now — here is where you are heading
scenes.body: The opening sets the frame. Next comes a live exercise, a demo, and then the first build block. Keep this board in view to stay oriented.
facilitatorNotes: Use this scene when you want to show that the participant surface is not decoration, but working orientation. [FACILITATING]
Name the three things participants should get in one glance: what is live now, what comes next, and what should exist in the repo.

blocks.title: Today you build so another team can continue
blocks.body: By the end of the day, a different team will read your repo without asking you. Start building for that moment now.

items.title: Context is King
items.body: A live contrast exercise that shows why context beats prompting.

items.title: Demo
items.body: A working agent workflow you will replicate in the build.

items.title: Build Phase 1
items.body: Your team builds in your repo until lunch. Milestones will appear on the board.

blocks.title: Important things belong in files
blocks.body: Not in conversation, not in your head. The afternoon handoff will test what actually made it into the repo.

phases.label: Context is King
phases.goal: Turn the opening energy into a precise thesis: harness engineering is team infrastructure for working with agents, and the first build move must begin with map, boundaries, and proof rather than another prompt.
phases.roomSummary: By the end of the talk, the room should see AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks, and explicit verification as working infrastructure rather than optional polish, and Build Phase 1 should feel like the obvious next move.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a repo and workflow where an agent and another team can continue safely.
If it is not in the repo, it does not exist.
When the agent does more, you need to verify better.
After this talk, teams should return to the repo with a concrete operating system, not with a search for a smarter prompt.
watchFors: The talk turns into a tool feature tour.
Teams hear prompting tips instead of workflow discipline.
There is no clean bridge into the first build phase.
checkpointQuestions: What will you move from your head or from chat into the repo today?
Which repo signal would help another team continue?
Which explicit check will be your trust boundary today?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make harness engineering precise, memorable, and immediately actionable so the room returns to the repo with a concrete first working contract.
say: Open with the repo-readiness contrast: same prompt, two repos. Let the room see the difference before you name it.
After the contrast, ask the room: 'What changed?' Let two voices answer.
Name what you saw: the agent in the bare repo made plausible but wrong decisions because it had no constraints. This is task drift.
Context is leverage, not cosmetics. The repo did the work, not the prompt.
show: Show the repo-readiness contrast first, then the thesis scene, then the bridge into build.
Use the participant surface only as proof that the room, dashboard, and repo still describe the same moment.
do: Run the contrast: show the bare repo variant first, then the harnessed variant with the same prompt.
After the contrast lands, transition to the thesis scene. Say the thesis once clearly.
Send the room into Build Phase 1 with one expectation: map and verification first, feature motion second.
watch: The talk must not collapse into prompt tips or tool theatrics.
By the end, the room should know what to do in the repo in the next few minutes.
fallback: If live demo drags, use pre-prepared screenshots. The contrast matters more than live generation.
If attention drops, keep the core line, the team-lead analogy, and one concrete build expectation.

scenes.label: Repo-readiness contrast
scenes.title: Same prompt. Different repo. Watch what happens.
scenes.body: This is a facilitator-led contrast. The same simple prompt runs in two repos: one bare, one with AGENTS.md and constraints. The prompt does not change. The repo does.
facilitatorNotes: This is a facilitator-led contrast. Do not narrate the conclusion before the room sees it. After showing both variants, pause and ask the room: 'What changed?' Let two voices answer before naming the thesis. [NARRATING]
Do not let this drift into a debate about which model is smarter. The point is that the repo context, not the prompt, changed the outcome.
Close with the line that Build Phase 1 starts here: map and verification first, feature motion second.

blocks.title: Same prompt. Different repo. Watch what happens.
blocks.body: One simple prompt. Two repos. The agent's output is only as good as the context it finds.

items.title: Variant A: bare repo
items.body: A simple prompt in a repo with no AGENTS.md, no constraints, no context. The agent drifts — plausible but wrong decisions.

items.title: Variant B: repo with harness
items.body: The same prompt in a repo with AGENTS.md (Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When), a plan, and a workshop skill. The agent produces aligned output.

blocks.title: The prompt did not change. The repo did.
blocks.body: Skilled practitioners do not put Goal, Context, Constraints, and Done When into prompts. They put it into the repo and use simple prompts. The harness does the work, not the wording.

scenes.label: Core line
scenes.title: What you just saw: context is leverage, not cosmetics
scenes.body: The contrast you just witnessed is the thesis in action. The repo context — not the prompt — determined whether the agent drifted or produced aligned work. Harness engineering is the discipline of making that context durable.
facilitatorNotes: The thesis arrives as the answer to a question the room already has. Say it once, clearly. Do not re-explain the contrast — the room saw it. [TEACHING]
Let the Lopopolo quote open the scene as a provocation the contrast just proved.
After the checklist, remind the room of the team-lead analogy and turn it directly into what teams should do next in the repo.

blocks.title: What you just saw: context is leverage, not cosmetics
blocks.body: The repo did the work, not the prompt. Harness engineering means shaping context, instructions, and workflow so the agent and the next team can carry the intent without verbal rescue.

blocks.title: What the team carries out of this room
items: Before generating the next feature, make the repo a place people can actually navigate.
When the agent does more, review and checks need to become sharper, not looser.
After the talk, return to the repo with a map and one explicit check instead of one more prompt idea.

scenes.label: Bridge into Build 1
scenes.title: After the talk, go back to the repo, not to more prompting
scenes.body: After the talk, the team should not leave with theory. It should align on the goal, write down the repo map, narrow the first slice, and name the first verifiable step. If the workshop skill is not installed yet, this is the moment.
facilitatorNotes: Use this briefly. This is not a dashboard tour. It is the operating contract for the first 10 to 15 minutes of Build Phase 1. [FACILITATING]
Name the expectation clearly: the team now returns to the repo for a map, a first slice, and one explicit check instead of more prompt debate.
Say explicitly: if the team does not have the workshop skill yet, now is the time for `harness skill install` and the first workshop command.

blocks.title: Go back to the repo with three things
blocks.body: A short repo map, the first working slice, and the smallest useful check. If the team does not have these three things after the talk, the real build work has not started yet.

blocks.title: What to do in the first minutes
items: Open README, AGENTS.md, and the brief. Align on goal, context, and constraints.
If the workshop skill is not installed yet, install it now: `harness skill install`, then `Codex: $workshop setup` or `pi: /skill:workshop`.
Write or tighten Goal, Context, Constraints, and Done When.
Pick the first slice that can actually be verified and add a check or tracer bullet immediately.

blocks.body: Build Phase 1 starts now. Align on what is live, what comes next, and what the team should verify before it asks the agent for more motion.

blocks.title: Bridge into Build 1
blocks.body: This is where theory ends. The team now needs to move the core line of the talk back into the repo as a map, a first decision log, and one verifiable step.

phases.label: Codex demo
phases.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
phases.roomSummary: The room should see quality rise as context, bounded next steps, and change control enter the flow. The point is not magic output, model mystique, or a feature parade.
facilitatorPrompts: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watchFors: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.
checkpointQuestions: What in this flow carries intent and constraints?
Where can you see that change control is part of the process?
What from this do you need to be able to build in your own repo?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Use one compact story to show a real working line: context, bounded next steps, implementation, change control, and explicit fallback moves when something goes wrong.
say: One story-driven example, not a feature list.
The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.
Review is not an emergency brake at the end. It is part of the workflow.
watch: The demo runs long and waits on generation.
It shows five modes of work instead of one clear line.
There are no explicit closure points after AGENTS.md, /plan, and /review.

scenes.label: Demo story
scenes.title: Not another prompt. A working system.
scenes.body: The demo story is intentionally ordinary: a developer gets a small task and chooses not to keep nudging the model blindly, but to make the repo show the goal, the next step, and how the change will be checked.
facilitatorNotes: Stay with one line. After 15 minutes, the audience should understand the workflow, not the tool menu. [NARRATING]

blocks.title: Not another prompt. A working system.
blocks.body: One compact story where you can watch context, bounded next steps, implementation, and change control reinforce each other.

blocks.title: The point of the demo
blocks.body: The tool alone is not enough. The working system around it is what matters.

scenes.label: Workflow steps
scenes.title: From AGENTS.md to review
scenes.body: The sequence should be simple enough for every table to repeat: without context it drifts, with AGENTS.md, bounded next steps, and change control it becomes a working system instead of another prompt.
facilitatorNotes: Have snapshots ready as fallbacks. Do not wait on generation for long. [TEACHING]
Do not drift into a feature tour or five different modalities.
After the callout, say the room-level point explicitly: quality rises fast when you add context, bounded next steps, and change control.
Fallbacks: if CLI is not working, switch to Codex App. If App is not working, use the web fallback. If demo is slow, have a repo snapshot after each step.

blocks.title: The workflow you want people to repeat

items.title: Without context the agent drifts
items.body: Briefly show a weak start without guardrails.

items.title: AGENTS.md with 4 elements
items.body: Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When.

items.title: Turn the work into bounded steps
items.body: Use `/plan` or a short step list to make the next move legible.

items.title: Small implementation slice + change control
items.body: Checks and review belong inside the workflow, not as panic recovery at the end.

blocks.title: What to take from the demo
blocks.body: The point is not a magical result. The point is that the repo makes clear why the work matters, what comes next, and how the change will be checked.

scenes.label: Participant demo board
scenes.title: Treat the demo as workflow, not a feature tour
scenes.body: During the demo, keep one working thread in view: where context came from, how the work was broken into steps, what got checked, and how the change came back under control.
facilitatorNotes: Use this when you want to remind the audience that the demo also needs a clear working line and a concrete learner payoff. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: Watch the working system, not only the output
blocks.body: During the demo, keep your eye on the invisible scaffolding: where context came from, where the next steps became clear, what got verified, and how review turned output into something safer to trust.

items.title: Where context comes from
items.body: AGENTS.md and the brief, not only a prompt blob.

items.title: Where the next step becomes clear
items.body: A short plan or step list should be visible before implementation starts.

items.title: Where the change is checked
items.body: Review and checks are part of the workflow, not an extra.

blocks.body: Watch how a small task turns into work another person could safely continue.
Next: you go back to the repo with three things — a map, a first slice, and the smallest useful check.

phases.label: Build fáze 1
phases.goal: Get every table to a real before-lunch baseline: a navigable repo, AGENTS.md, a plan, one explicit check, and a first reviewed slice of work.
phases.roomSummary: The facilitator should keep the milestone board visible, coach through questions, and keep pushing teams away from unbounded generation and toward explicit verification.
facilitatorPrompts: Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.
If the team has no verification at all, push for the smallest useful test or tracer bullet.
Return learner-facing artifacts to participants, not the whole backstage of Harness Lab.
watchFors: The team keeps generating text, but verifies nothing.
AGENTS.md grows into a warehouse for everything.
It is unclear what is done, what is in progress, and what is only a hypothesis.
checkpointQuestions: Does the team have one shared understanding of the goal?
Is context accumulating in the repo, or staying in heads and chat?
Could another team find the first safe move in five minutes?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Get every table to a real before-lunch baseline: a navigable repo, AGENTS.md, a plan, one explicit check, and a first reviewed slice of work.
say: Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.
If the team has no verification at all, push for the smallest useful test or tracer bullet.
Return learner-facing artifacts to participants, not the whole backstage of Harness Lab.
An agent that starts without constraints will make plausible but wrong architectural decisions. This is task drift. AGENTS.md constraints are the fix.
watch: The team keeps generating text, but verifies nothing.
AGENTS.md grows into a warehouse for everything.
It is unclear what is done, what is in progress, and what is only a hypothesis.

scenes.label: Milestone board
scenes.title: What should be in the repo before lunch
scenes.body: A README, a short map in AGENTS.md, a step-by-step plan, a runnable check, a first verified move, and a working slice another team could actually run. If one of those is missing, the team is not building a working system yet.
facilitatorNotes: This is the main room cue for Build Phase 1. Keep it on the projector often. [COACHING]

blocks.title: Six things before lunch
items: A README explains the project to a new reader.
AGENTS.md shows where to start and where to reach next.
A plan holds the line instead of improvisation.
A build/test command or tracer bullet puts the work back on the ground.
The repo shows a first actually verified move.
A first working slice another team could run — one command, one screen, one path that proves the system works.

blocks.title: More autonomy, more evidence
blocks.body: When the agent does more work independently, the team has to raise the quality of checks and repo hygiene.

scenes.label: When a team gets stuck
scenes.title: When a team gets stuck, return to the proof
scenes.body: First aid is not a longer prompt or a longer explanation. It is a return to the goal, the missing repo artifact, and the smallest check that anchors the work back in reality.
facilitatorNotes: Good help is not a verbal handoff. It is returning the team to the repo, checks, and the next safe move. [COACHING]
Keep the order coach -> mentor -> teacher. If you explain for too long, return the team to work quickly.

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: What to look for while circulating

blocks.title: Before you try another round of generation

items.title: State what you are trying to prove
items.body: Make sure it is clear what the team is trying to do and how they will know they actually moved forward.

items.title: Find the blind spot in the repo
items.body: Is the problem in the README, AGENTS.md, plan, runbook, or repo structure?

items.title: Add the smallest check
items.body: A test, tracer bullet, or another executable signal moves the work from guessing back to evidence.

blocks.title: What another team should already be able to read
items: The team has one shared understanding of the goal.
Context grows in the repo, not only in conversation.
A test, tracer bullet, or another explicit check exists.
From the repo, you can tell what is done and what the next safe move is.

blocks.title: An answer is not the same thing as help
blocks.body: If the team gets a spoken answer without artifacts and without a check, the problem only goes underground. Return the work to the repo and to a verifiable next move.

scenes.label: Participant board
scenes.title: In the first build, keep five things together
scenes.body: This is not the moment for another clever prompt. This is the moment to make sure your repo setup can carry the next move without table-side improvisation.
facilitatorNotes: Use this when you want to return a learner-facing view to the team instead of facilitator backstage. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: By lunch, you need a repo map, step plan, runnable check, and first move
blocks.body: Keep a short AGENTS.md as a map, a short step plan, an executable check, and a first verified move in view. If you lose orientation, come back to those four things first.

blocks.title: Keep in frame now
items: A README and AGENTS.md people can actually navigate.
A short step plan and the nearest safe move.
A first check that separates reality from confidence.
A note another team can continue from.
A first working slice you can demo to another team in under a minute.

blocks.body: The next shared beat is a short intermezzo. Arrive with one sentence: what you changed and why.

blocks.title: If you get stuck
blocks.body: Return to the README, AGENTS.md, the workshop skill, the plan, and the smallest useful verification. Unbounded generation will slow you down.

phases.label: Intermezzo 1
phases.goal: Make the first round of learning visible across the room and reconnect teams to the discipline behind the output, not only to the output itself.
phases.roomSummary: Each team gives one tight signal. The facilitator then connects repo evidence, room observations, and one principle point grounded in what is actually happening.
facilitatorPrompts: What did you change and why?
What did you move from chat or from your head into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
watchFors: The intermezzo slips into reporting without a principle point.
Only the loudest team speaks while the others stay passive.
The facilitator summarizes impressions instead of evidence.
checkpointQuestions: What did you move from your head or chat into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
What should the next team read first?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Make the first round of learning visible across the room and reconnect teams to the discipline behind the output, not only to the output itself.
say: What did you change and why?
What did you move from chat or from your head into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes team discussion, then room sharing.
watch: The intermezzo slips into reporting without a principle point.
Only the loudest team speaks while the others stay passive.
The facilitator summarizes impressions instead of evidence.
If someone starts talking during the silent phase, redirect. The retrieval benefit requires individual production before social sharing.

scenes.label: Silent retrieval
scenes.title: 3 minutes. Write before you speak.
scenes.body: Each person writes individually. No sharing yet.
facilitatorNotes: Enforce the silence. If anyone starts discussing, redirect: 'Write first, then we'll share.' After 3 minutes, open team discussion for 2 minutes, then room sharing. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: 3 minutes. Write before you speak.
blocks.body: Each person writes individually. No sharing yet.

blocks.title: The question
blocks.body: What would the next team need to find in your repo right now to continue without asking you?

scenes.label: Checkpoint board
scenes.title: Intermezzo: evidence, not impressions
scenes.body: Keep the intermezzo disciplined: one sentence from each team, then the shared signals, then one principle point tied to real evidence rather than facilitator vibes.
facilitatorNotes: Keep it short. The intermezzo is a reset and checkpoint, not a long presentation. [FACILITATING]

items.title: Teams: one sentence
items.body: What we changed and why.

items.title: Shared signal summary
items.body: What is visible at the tables, in the repo, and in monitoring.

items.title: One principle point
items.body: Tied to what is actually visible in the repo and the workflow today.

blocks.title: Checkpoint questions
items: What did you move from chat or from your head into the repo?
What are you verifying today with an executable check?
What should the next team read first?

scenes.label: Participant intermezzo board
scenes.title: In the intermezzo, listen for three signals
scenes.body: This is not a status report. It is a short checkpoint that should show what is actually changing in the repo and what is still only talk.
facilitatorNotes: This scene keeps the intermezzo as a short checkpoint for the whole room. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: We collect signals, not impressions
blocks.body: The short intermezzo should help every table notice what is actually changing in the repo and in the way the teams are working.

blocks.title: Notice especially
items: What changed and why.
What moved from people’s heads into the repo.
What is actually being verified today.

blocks.body: Take one thing from this intermezzo that you will verify before the next checkpoint.

phases.label: Lunch and handoff prep
phases.goal: Interrupt the momentum before the afternoon shift and force teams to leave behind a repo another group can actually read.
phases.roomSummary: Before lunch, teams need to clean up context, record the next safe move, and leave the repo in a state that does not rely on their memory. Lunch is not a pause in the handoff constraint.
facilitatorPrompts: Handoff is not the end of the day. It is a design constraint throughout the day.
Write down what the next team should read first.
If the same rule was said aloud twice, it belongs in the repo.
watchFors: Teams leave for lunch without a clear next step and without cleaned-up context.
A key decision is still only in people’s heads.
The repo does not explain the midday state.
checkpointQuestions: What should the next team read first?
Does the repo show what is done, what is a hypothesis, and what the next safe move is?
What still lives only in conversation and must go into the repo?

sourceRefs.label: Talk: What I want them to adopt

facilitatorRunner.goal: Interrupt the momentum before the afternoon shift and force teams to leave behind a repo another group can actually read.
say: Handoff is not the end of the day. It is a design constraint throughout the day.
Write down what the next team should read first.
If the same rule was said aloud twice, it belongs in the repo.
do: During lunch, quietly check each team repo. If a repo is genuinely unusable (no AGENTS.md at all, no code, broken setup), help the team write the minimum. Frame it as normal coaching, not a gate.
watch: Teams leave for lunch without a clear next step and without cleaned-up context.
A key decision is still only in people’s heads.
The repo does not explain the midday state.
The gate is silent — participants should not know there is a check. Only intervene on genuinely broken repos, not weak ones.

scenes.label: Lunch reset
scenes.title: Lunch is not a break from handoff
scenes.body: Before anyone leaves for lunch, the repo has to show what changed, what verifies it, and what the next safe move is for the incoming team.
facilitatorNotes: This is quiet handoff prep. Explain it as part of the work, not as admin overhead. [FACILITATING]

sourceRefs.label: Talk: What I want them to adopt

blocks.title: Before you step away
items: The next safe move is written down.
Key rules and decisions are in the repo.
It is clear what is done and what is only a hypothesis.
The next team knows what to read first.

scenes.label: Participant lunch reset board
scenes.title: Do not leave without the next safe move
scenes.body: Before lunch, you are preparing the repo for a different team. What happens next, what is done, and what is still only a hypothesis should all be readable without you.
facilitatorNotes: Use this when you want to remind teams that lunch is not a break from handoff. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: Prepare the repo for the afternoon handoff
blocks.body: Before lunch, the repo has to make four things obvious: what changed, what is done, what is still only a hypothesis, and how the next team should continue.

blocks.title: Before you step away
items: The next safe move is written down.
Key decisions are in the repo.
It is clear what is done and what is still a hypothesis.
The next team knows what to open first.

blocks.body: When you return, a different team will read your repo without asking you. What will they find?

phases.label: Team rotation
phases.goal: Force a quiet start after rotation and let repo quality reveal what is genuinely legible.
phases.roomSummary: For the first ten minutes, the incoming team only reads the repo and maps the situation. Any confusion is diagnostic data about the quality of the context, not a workshop bug to be patched with talk.
facilitatorPrompts: The first 10 minutes are quiet. No improvised explanation.
For the first 10 minutes, the new team only reads the repo and maps the situation.
Frustration is not a workshop bug. It is a signal of context quality in the repository.
watchFors: The original team starts explaining things verbally.
The new team edits the first file they open without a situation map.
The facilitator rescues a weak repo signal with spoken handoff.
checkpointQuestions: What helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what is the next safe move?
Does the repo make it clear where to look first?
What needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Force a quiet start after rotation and let repo quality reveal what is genuinely legible.
say: The first 10 minutes are quiet. No improvised explanation.
For the first 10 minutes, the new team only reads the repo and maps the situation.
Frustration is not a workshop bug. It is a signal of context quality in the repository.
A new agent session has no memory of the previous session. If your context is in chat only, the session starts blind. This is why the repo is the session state.
watch: The original team starts explaining things verbally.
The new team edits the first file they open without a situation map.
The facilitator rescues a weak repo signal with spoken handoff.

scenes.label: Rotation framing
scenes.title: Quiet start after rotation
scenes.body: For the first ten minutes, the incoming team only reads the repo and builds its own picture of the situation. Do not rescue the system with spoken explanation; let the repo show its quality honestly.
facilitatorNotes: Be strict. The moment you allow improvised explanation, the whole point of the afternoon is lost. [NARRATING]

blocks.title: Quiet start after rotation
blocks.body: The repo has to show where to start now.

blocks.title: The first 10 minutes
blocks.body: Only read, map the situation, and write your own diagnosis.

scenes.label: Instructions for the new team
scenes.title: After rotation, the first job is not coding
scenes.body: Start with the README, AGENTS.md, and the plan. First make a map: what helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what the next safe move is.
facilitatorNotes: If the team does not know what to reach for, return them to learner kit artifacts. [COACHING]
Make sure the team's own diagnosis appears before the first edit.

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Instructions for the new team

blocks.title: The first 10 minutes for the new team

items.title: Start with the README, AGENTS.md, and the plan
items.body: Do not skip the repo map.

items.title: Write your own diagnosis
items.body: What helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what is the next safe move?

items.title: Only then change code
items.body: Do not reward confusion by editing the first file you see.

items.title: When unsure, return to the learner kit
items.body: Template-agents, reference, analyze-checklist, and challenge cards should be the first support, not verbal rescue.

blocks.title: If you do not know where to start
blocks.body: Return to the learner kit and to the guiding artifacts in the repo. A weak signal should not be rescued by an improvised briefing.

scenes.label: Participant handoff board
scenes.title: After rotation, do not code for ten minutes
scenes.body: The incoming team should first read the repo, write its own diagnosis, and only then touch code. If that feels hard, the problem is in the signal, not in your discipline.
facilitatorNotes: This is the participant equivalent of the hard handoff moment. It helps keep the room disciplined without backstage details. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: The new team starts with a map, not improvisation
blocks.body: Read the README, AGENTS.md, and the plan first. Then write what helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what the next safe move is.

items.title: Read the repo map
items.body: Start with the README, AGENTS.md, and the plan.

items.title: Write your own diagnosis
items.body: What helps, what is missing, what is risky, and what is the next safe move?

items.title: Only then change things
items.body: Do not start editing without a shared situation map.

blocks.body: If the signal is weak, do not patch it with talk. Write the diagnosis down and fix the map, rule, or check.

phases.label: Build fáze 2
phases.goal: Continue from repo-native signals only, then turn the friction of the handoff into stronger maps, stronger checks, and stronger continuation guidance.
phases.roomSummary: The second build phase is not simply more implementation. It is the test of whether the repo and the agent workflow can carry a genuinely new team.
facilitatorPrompts: Do not let teams replace a weak repo signal with verbal handoff.
Name what needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation.
Every repeated pain is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook.
watchFors: The team only cleans up chaos, but does not turn it into better guidance.
A weak repo signal keeps getting rescued in conversation.
There is no explicit new verification after the handoff.
checkpointQuestions: Which repo signal helped you continue the most?
What do you need to write or clarify after rotation?
Which next check do you need now so you can continue safely?

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Facilitation point for rotation

facilitatorRunner.goal: Continue from repo-native signals only, then turn the friction of the handoff into stronger maps, stronger checks, and stronger continuation guidance.
say: Do not let teams replace a weak repo signal with verbal handoff.
Name what needs to be written, clarified, or verified after rotation.
Every repeated pain is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook.

When the agent generates tests as part of the same task, those tests reflect the agent interpretation, not your specification. Independent verification means: you wrote the done criteria, or a separate check evaluates the output.
do: Start with the codification pause. 5 minutes. Only after teams have encoded at least one friction point do they continue building.
watch: The team only cleans up chaos, but does not turn it into better guidance.
A weak repo signal keeps getting rescued in conversation.
There is no explicit new verification after the handoff.

scenes.label: Codification pause
scenes.title: Before you write code: name one friction and encode it
scenes.body: 5 minutes. Name what caused friction in the rotation. Write it into the repo.
facilitatorNotes: This is the trigger for Habits 5 and 6. Enforce the 5-minute pause. Teams will want to jump to coding — redirect them to the encoding step first. [COACHING]

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Facilitation point for rotation

blocks.title: Before you write code: name one friction and encode it
blocks.body: 5 minutes. Name what caused friction in the rotation. Write it into the repo.

blocks.title: Encode, then continue
blocks.body: Write it as a rule, a check, or a clearer AGENTS.md section. Only after it is written do you move to feature work.

scenes.label: After rotation: keep building
scenes.title: After rotation, fix the signal as well as the feature
scenes.body: Build Phase 2 should turn handoff friction into a better repo map, stronger checks, and a clearer next safe move. Do not continue as if nothing happened.
facilitatorNotes: Help the team see frustration as a diagnostic signal, not a failure. [COACHING]
Ask what genuinely helped after rotation, what had to be written down, and which next check now keeps the work grounded.

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Facilitation point for rotation

blocks.title: Friction is diagnostics
blocks.body: When the new team struggles, look for hidden context or missing verification. Do not fix the problem by talking around the repo.

items.title: Name what truly helped continuation
items.body: Capture the signal in the repo, not just the feeling.

items.title: Write what is missing or unclear
items.body: Tighten the map, a decision, or the handoff artifact.

items.title: Add the next explicit check
items.body: Before the next bigger move, put the work back on verification rails.

scenes.label: Participant continuation board
scenes.title: After rotation, fix the signal first, then the feature
scenes.body: This is not only about finishing the task. It is about leaving the repo more legible, more verifiable, and cheaper for the next takeover than it was when you arrived.
facilitatorNotes: This is the participant equivalent of the second build phase: no rescue through talking, only better orientation and the next check. [FACILITATING]

sourceRefs.label: Facilitation guide: Facilitation point for rotation

blocks.title: Continue only from what is traceable
blocks.body: Name what helped you continue, write down the missing signal, and add the next explicit check before you make a larger change.

blocks.title: Do this now
items: Name what in the repo helped you continue.
Write down what is still unclear or missing after rotation.
Add the next explicit verification before a larger change.

blocks.body: Every repeated afternoon pain is a candidate for a better map, rule, challenge card, or runbook.

phases.label: Intermezzo 2
phases.goal: Capture what truly helped after rotation and what is emerging as a weak point in the room’s current way of working.
phases.roomSummary: The second intermezzo should pull concrete continuation signals into the open and prepare the room for reveal and reflection without drifting into vague retrospectives.
facilitatorPrompts: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing or slowing you down?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?
watchFors: The intermezzo stays general and without concrete examples.
You collect opinions, not signals from the repo.
No link appears to the closing reflection.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Capture what truly helped after rotation and what is emerging as a weak point in the room’s current way of working.
say: What helped you continue after rotation?
What was missing or slowing you down?
Which repo signal saved you the most time?
do: Start with the silent retrieval scene. 3 minutes individual writing, then 2 minutes team discussion, then room sharing.
watch: The intermezzo stays general and without concrete examples.
You collect opinions, not signals from the repo.
No link appears to the closing reflection.
If someone starts talking during the silent phase, redirect. The retrieval benefit requires individual production before social sharing.

scenes.label: Silent retrieval
scenes.title: 3 minutes. Write before you speak.
scenes.body: Each person writes individually. No sharing yet.
facilitatorNotes: Enforce the silence. If anyone starts discussing, redirect: 'Write first, then we'll share.' After 3 minutes, open team discussion for 2 minutes, then room sharing. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: 3 minutes. Write before you speak.
blocks.body: Each person writes individually. No sharing yet.

blocks.title: The question
blocks.body: Name one thing that genuinely helped you continue after rotation, and one that was missing.

scenes.label: Continuation signals
scenes.title: What really helped people continue after rotation
scenes.body: Use this moment to pull out hard continuation signals: what sped the new team up, what slowed it down, and what now deserves to become a better template, runbook, or rule.
facilitatorNotes: Ask for concrete repo examples, not general impressions. [FACILITATING]
Prepare the room for reveal by separating genuinely useful signals from good vibes.

items.title: The signal that truly helped
items.body: What saved time or reduced risk for the incoming team.

items.title: The signal that was missing
items.body: What stayed hidden in heads, chat, or an unverified state.

items.title: The candidate for codification
items.body: What should become a better template, check, or rule after today.

blocks.title: Evidence, not impressions
blocks.body: Every answer should point to something traceable in the repo or to a clearly missing signal that should have been there.

scenes.label: Participant continuation signals board
scenes.title: Prepare one thing that really helped after rotation
scenes.body: This is not a broad mood check about the afternoon. It is a search for what saved time for the incoming team and what still blocked it.
facilitatorNotes: This scene helps pull the afternoon back to concrete continuation signals before reveal. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: We are collecting continuation signals
blocks.body: We are not only asking what felt good. We are asking which repo signals actually helped the new team continue.

blocks.title: Notice
items: What helped people continue after rotation.
What was missing or slowing them down.
Which signal saved the most time.

blocks.body: Reveal is next. Bring one thing you want to carry into next week.

phases.label: Reveal and reflection
phases.goal: Close the day by naming the signals that helped work survive handoff and by turning them into next practice, not just a pleasant ending.
phases.roomSummary: Reveal and reflection are not a team ranking. They are the moment where the room decides which signals helped work survive handoff and which ones repeatedly failed.
facilitatorPrompts: We are not judging which team was better.
We are looking at the system: which signals help work survive handoff and which ones slow it down.
Collect concrete examples, not general impressions.
watchFors: The reflection turns into ranking teams.
The questions stay too general and detached from the repo.
No next step emerges for next week or for improving the blueprint.
checkpointQuestions: What helped you continue?
What was missing?
What will you do differently next week?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Close the day by naming the signals that helped work survive handoff and by turning them into next practice, not just a pleasant ending.
say: We are not judging which team was better.
We are looking at the system: which signals help work survive handoff and which ones slow it down.
Collect concrete examples, not general impressions.
These practices are tool-agnostic. If you are back in a different toolchain Monday, the principles transfer: context in tracked artifacts, plan before motion, verification before trust.
do: After W3, run the Monday commitments: each person writes one sentence. Collect them.
watch: The reflection turns into ranking teams.
The questions stay too general and detached from the repo.
No next step emerges for next week or for improving the blueprint.

scenes.label: 1-2-4-All
scenes.title: Only take out what you want to repeat in real work
scenes.body: Use the final 1-2-4-All to surface the concrete signals that helped work survive handoff, and to separate them from general feelings about the day.
facilitatorNotes: Keep the questions concrete. Every example should point to a signal in the repo or to a missing signal. [FACILITATING]
During the group share, keep steering away from team scoring and back toward system signals.

items.title: 1 minute solo
items.body: Write what helped continuation, what was missing, and which signal saved the most time.

items.title: 2 minutes in pairs
items.body: Compare examples and choose one that is really worth sharing.

items.title: 4 minutes in fours
items.body: Look for repeated signals, not for better or worse teams.

items.title: All
items.body: Share only concrete repo signals and concrete missing signals.

blocks.title: Do not take away impressions. Take away rules.
blocks.body: We are not asking who was better. We are asking which signals help work survive handoff and which ones still break under pressure.

scenes.label: W3 closeout
scenes.title: What from today belongs in your real repo tomorrow
scenes.body: W3 matters because it forces the day to end in concrete future practice rather than in abstract agreement.
facilitatorNotes: At the end, turn it toward next week as well as toward improving the blueprint and workshop system. [COACHING]

items.title: What?
items.body: What happened today, without judgment.

items.title: So what?
items.body: What that means for working with AI agents.

items.title: Now what?
items.body: What you will do differently next week.

blocks.title: Turn pain into an artifact
blocks.body: Every repeated pain point is a candidate for a better template, challenge card, runbook, or piece of blueprint guidance.

scenes.label: Monday commitment
scenes.title: Write one sentence you will act on Monday
scenes.body: Not an aspiration. A concrete move.
facilitatorNotes: Collect the written commitments (cards, repo commits, or shared doc). Push for specificity: if someone writes something vague, ask 'What is the first concrete move that starts this?' [COACHING]

blocks.title: Write one sentence you will act on Monday
blocks.body: Not an aspiration. A concrete move.

items.title: Next week I will [specific action] because [specific reason from today].

blocks.title: Be specific enough to check
blocks.body: Not: 'I will work better with agents.' Yes: 'I will add Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When to my main repo's AGENTS.md before my next agent task.'

blocks.title: The harness is the repo, not the tool
blocks.body: These practices work with any coding agent — Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot. What you learned today is tool-agnostic.

scenes.label: Participant closeout board
scenes.title: Leave with three things, not only a good feeling
scenes.body: At the end of the day, take one signal you want to keep, one weakness you do not want to repeat, and one move for next week.
facilitatorNotes: Use this scene when you pull the day back to concrete signals and next steps. [FACILITATING]

blocks.title: This is not about winners. It is about the signals that hold work together.
blocks.body: At the end of the day, we look at what helped the next team continue, what was missing, and what we will change in our own repos next week.

blocks.title: Carry out mainly
items: One signal you want to install permanently.
One weakness you will write down earlier next time.
One concrete move for next week.

blocks.body: The recap and follow-up package should continue this right after the workshop. Do not leave the commitment only on a sticky note.

briefs.title: Code Review Helper
briefs.problem: Code review quality swings with whoever's looking. Some changes arrive with a sharp checklist and clear risk framing; others come through unevenly and the team loses consistency. Your job: design a tool that turns a diff into a usable review checklist — one that says what it is certain about, what is heuristic suggestion, and what still needs human judgment.
userStories: As a reviewer, I want a fast checklist of risks, questions, and focus areas from a diff.
As the author of a change, I want to know what I should verify before the review starts.
As the team after rotation, I want to continue from the heuristics the original team already discovered instead of reinventing them.
architectureNotes: This can be a CLI, web app, or simple script. The important part is a clear `diff -> analysis -> checklist` flow.
It must be obvious which inputs the tool expects and what it cannot evaluate reliably.
Add a seed diff or `examples/` so the workflow can be checked locally.
Separate heuristic from certainty clearly. The tool should help the reviewer, not pretend to be infallible.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can add a new review rule within 10 minutes without reading through the whole codebase. *(Handoff test.)*
The tool produces a review checklist from a seed diff.
The output clearly separates certain findings from heuristic suggestions from items still needing human judgment.
The review rules themselves are readable and editable by someone who didn't write them.
There is at least one concrete example in `examples/` that demonstrates the full flow.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. First write the review rules, define what a good checklist looks like for a specific seed diff, and clarify what the tool *cannot* evaluate reliably. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: DevToolbox CLI
briefs.problem: Every team accumulates small one-off scripts — log cleaners, JSON parsers, commit lookups — that work for one person until nobody remembers how to run them. Your job: design a CLI that solves a few real developer pain points and survives handoff. Not a bag of scripts. A small system where it's obvious where the next command, test, and doc belong.
userStories: As a developer, I want to turn a log or JSON blob into a readable format with one command.
As a developer, I want to quickly find suspicious commits, branches, or changes without manually assembling git commands.
As a team, I want both the commands and the way of working documented so another team can continue after rotation without confusion.
architectureNotes: Choose any language or framework, but the CLI must stay easy to run and easy to discover.
Separate commands from helper utilities and configuration from the start.
`AGENTS.md` should describe the build and test flow, output conventions, and the rules for future extension.
A runbook for the team that inherits the project after lunch matters as much as a working command.
Do not build a bag of scripts. Build a small system where it is obvious where the next command, test, and doc belong.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can add or fix a command within 10 minutes of opening the repo. *(Handoff test.)*
At least 3 working commands, each solving a concrete developer pain point.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run and verify locally.
The extension pattern is visible — a new command fits without breaking the structure.
Every command has at least one readable input/output example.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with `AGENTS.md`, a short plan for the extension pattern, and one clear verification step. Only then implement the first command.

briefs.title: Doc Generator
briefs.problem: Documentation goes stale almost immediately. Once maintenance is fully manual, the team postpones it, and after a few iterations nobody knows whether the docs still describe reality. After handoff, the next team no longer knows what to trust and what is only inference. Your job: design a tool that generates baseline technical documentation — or a structured project overview — from an existing codebase in a way that makes *what we know* and *what we're inferring* visibly different.
userStories: As a developer, I want basic technical documentation from a project without writing everything from scratch.
As a reviewer, I want to understand the module structure and main entry points within minutes.
As the team after rotation, I want to discover the architecture without long detective work.
architectureNotes: The input can be a local repo, seed directory, or simplified dataset.
The output can be Markdown, HTML, or a simple text report.
The important part is to explain what the tool can infer reliably and what remains heuristic.
Design the structure from the start so another output type can be added later.
Do not optimize for AI theatre. Optimize for trust, traceability, and a clear next safe move for a team after handoff.
acceptanceCriteria: A reviewer can read the generated documentation and tell exactly where each claim came from — within minutes, without opening the original source. *(Handoff test — provenance is the whole point.)*
Another team can add a new output format (Markdown → HTML, report → overview) without refactoring the whole tool.
The generated output makes it obvious which claims are *certain* (read directly from source) and which are *inference* (heuristic).
The tool produces at least one complete, readable output for a real or seed codebase.
`README` and `AGENTS.md` explain how to run locally and what input the tool expects.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Start by listing every signal the tool will read from a codebase (file names, imports, commit messages, directory structure, comments — whatever you're choosing) and for each one, decide whether it produces *certain* output or *heuristic* output. Write that spec in `AGENTS.md`. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

briefs.title: Metrics Dashboard
briefs.problem: Every team has the data — user signups, deploy frequency, error rates, feature usage — scattered across different tools. The shared screen that turns it into a decision-making view usually doesn't exist. Your job: design a dashboard that puts a few metrics and one trend on one screen, stays cleanly separated between data and UI, and is still legible after handoff to a team that didn't build it.
userStories: As a team, I want several metrics on one screen so the current state is legible quickly.
As a facilitator, I want to change seed data without touching UI logic.
As the team after rotation, I want to understand the data structure, components, and screens within minutes.
architectureNotes: Separate seed data from UI from the first commit.
Mobile-first is an advantage, but the dashboard must remain readable on a projected screen too.
README and monitoring should explain what already works, what is still mock, and what is still missing.
Design the structure so adding another metric does not require rewriting the whole screen.
Do not optimize only for the look. Make the data model, layout rules, and verification path easy for another team to understand.
acceptanceCriteria: A team that didn't build this can add a new metric within 10 minutes without breaking the layout. *(Handoff test.)*
The dashboard shows at least 3 metrics and one trend or comparison.
Seed data and UI logic are cleanly separated — a facilitator can swap seed data without touching the UI.
The layout stays legible on mobile and on a large projected screen; the `README` says how to test both.
The `README` documents what's real, what's mocked, and what's missing.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with the UI. Start with the data model, the component boundaries, the layout rules, and a `Done When` in `AGENTS.md`. Only then touch any visual code.

briefs.title: Standup Bot
briefs.problem: Daily standups in chat become long threads where blockers get lost, dependencies between people are invisible, and nothing is reconstructable an hour later. Your job: design a tool that turns standup inputs into an overview a different team can continue working from — without the original author, without the original thread, and without a verbal handoff.
userStories: As a team lead, I want standup responses collected into one readable summary.
As a developer, I want to quickly see blockers, dependencies, and topics that need coordination.
As the team after rotation, I want to understand the data flow and integration points without verbal handoff.
architectureNotes: Prefer a clear data model over complicated integration.
Mock data is fine if the workflow feels realistic and is documented clearly.
Separate ingest, processing, and presentation of the output.
Prompts, runbooks, and decisions must live in the repo, not only in the heads of the original team.
Do not optimize for pretty summary prose before blockers, dependencies, and the next safe move are visible.
acceptanceCriteria: Another team can continue this project without a verbal explanation from the original team. *(Handoff test.)*
The tool ingests seed data in a documented format and produces an overview that surfaces blockers, dependencies, and items needing coordination.
The output distinguishes what the tool is certain about from what is only a heuristic suggestion.
The repo explains how to connect the ingest to a real input channel — without requiring it.
Ingest, processing, and presentation are cleanly separated in the code.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Don't start with code. Start with: the seed data format, the output data model, the rules that distinguish certainty from heuristic, and an `AGENTS.md` a rotating team will open first. Only then propose the first implementation slice.

challenges.title: Create AGENTS.md as a map
challenges.description: Write down the goal, build and test flow, durable rules, and where the next team should look first.

challenges.title: Write a code review skill
challenges.description: At least one repeatable review routine must live in the repo as a skill or runbook, not only in a prompt.

challenges.title: Use /plan before coding
challenges.description: Let the agent plan the work first, show what the plan was based on, and record the next safe move.

challenges.title: Add the smallest useful verification
challenges.description: Create a RED test, tracer bullet, or simple browser check before you give the agent more autonomy.

challenges.title: Run 2 parallel Codex sessions
challenges.description: Split the problem into two independent streams and compare what actually worked.

challenges.title: Add 'Done When' to every task
challenges.description: Every important task needs an explicit completion criterion and a link to verification.
